The speed improvement of the JKOnet∗ models family suggests that a theoretically guided loss may provide strong computational benefits on par with sophisticated model architectures.
Our linearly parametrized models, JKOnet∗l and JKOnet∗l,V, require a computational time per epoch comparable to the JKOnet family, but they only need one epoch to solve the problem optimally.
Our non-linear models, JKOnet∗ and JKOnet∗V, instead both require significantly lower time per epoch and converge faster than the JKOnet family.

Compared to JKOnet, our model also requires a simpler architecture: we drop the additional ICNN used in the inner iteration and the related training details (e.g., the strong convexity regularizer and the teacher forcing).

Notice that simply replacing the ICNN in JKOnet with a vanilla MLP deprives the method of the theoretical connections with optimal transport, which, in our experiments, appears to be associated with stability (NaN in Figure 3).

JKOnet∗ is introduced as a model based on the JKO scheme for diffusion processes.

The complex bilevel optimization problem is replaced with a simple mean-square error, resulting in outperforming existing methods in terms of computational cost, solution accuracy, and expressiveness.
In the prediction of cellular processes, JKOnet∗ achieves state-of-the-art performance.
JKOnet∗ trains in less than a minute compared to the hours required by all existing methods.
The work has limitations and did not address a few important challenges, which are seen as exciting open questions.
JKOnet∗ performance is attributed to a loss function motivated by deep theoretical results.
The architecture of JKOnet∗ is described as 'vanilla' and was not thoroughly investigated in this work.
The choice of features for the linear parametrization was not extensively investigated in this work, despite displaying promising results in experiments.
Known failure modes are further discussed in Appendix G.

The approach followed in this work is expected to apply to other exciting avenues of applied machine learning research, including population steering, reinforcement learning, diffusion models, and transformers.
